mechanism_narrative: The federal funds rate at 0.08% represents an extremely accommodative monetary stance. This ultra-low rate environment reduces the discount rate applied to future corporate earnings, directly increasing equity valuations. S&P 500 forward returns are positive when FEDFUNDS remains below 1.0% over the 20-day horizon.
failure_condition: S&P 500 produces negative 20-day returns in more than 45% of periods when FEDFUNDS < 1.0%, or Rank IC < 0.02 over any 2-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: Rising payroll employment (PAYEMS) signals robust labor market strength and consumer income growth, which drives aggregate demand and corporate profit expectations. A month-over-month increase in PAYEMS of more than 100,000 persons predicts positive S&P 500 returns over the next 5 trading days as forward-looking equity investors price in sustained economic momentum.
candidate_trigger: PAYEMS increases by more than 100,000 persons in the prior month
evidence_refs: fact:PAYEMS at 137,802 thousand suggests stable employment
regime_hint: late-cycle expansion with labor market tightening
failure_condition: Positive 5-day returns occur in fewer than 55% of months when PAYEMS increases > 100,000, or correlation between PAYEMS growth and 5-day SPX returns is not statistically significant (p > 0.10).

mechanism_narrative: Retail sales (RSAFS) directly reflect consumer spending, the largest component of GDP. When RSAFS declines month-over-month, it signals weakening aggregate demand and reduced corporate revenue expectations. This triggers negative S&P 500 returns over the subsequent 20-day period as equity investors reprice earnings downward.
failure_condition: S&P 500 produces positive 20-day returns in more than 50% of instances when RSAFS declines > 1.5%, or Spearman rank correlation between RSAFS growth and forward returns is not significantly negative (p > 0.10).

mechanism_narrative: Industrial production (INDPRO) measures real economic output capacity utilization. When INDPRO growth decelerates below 0.5% month-over-month while remaining positive, it signals a supply-side slowdown that constrains profit margins. This interaction with stable but not accelerating demand produces negative equity returns over 60 trading days as investors anticipate margin compression.
candidate_trigger: INDPRO growth falls below 0.5% month-over-month while RSAFS remains flat or positive
failure_condition: 60-day forward returns are positive in more than 55% of triggered instances, or the joint effect of INDPRO and RSAFS on returns is not statistically significant (interaction p-value > 0.15).

mechanism_narrative: Housing starts (HOUST) are a leading indicator of residential investment and construction employment. When HOUST declines below 900 thousand units annualized, it signals weakening forward demand for construction materials, labor, and related services. This predicts negative S&P 500 returns over the next 60 trading days as the market reprices cyclical sector earnings.
evidence_refs: fact:HOUST at 974.0 thousand units, above 900 threshold
regime_hint: housing cycle downturn or demand deceleration
failure_condition: S&P 500 produces positive 60-day returns in more than 50% of periods when HOUST < 900, or Rank IC between HOUST and forward returns is below 0.04 over any 3-year window.

mechanism_narrative: Inflation measured by CPIAUCSL rising above 3.0% year-over-year forces the Federal Reserve to tighten monetary policy, raising FEDFUNDS. The lag between inflation acceleration and policy response creates a window where real rates are negative but nominal rates are rising, causing equity volatility and negative returns. This mechanism operates over a 20-day horizon during the tightening transition.
candidate_trigger: CPIAUCSL year-over-year growth exceeds 3.0% while FEDFUNDS remains below 2.0%
failure_condition: S&P 500 produces positive 20-day returns in more than 60% of triggered instances, or the interaction term between CPIAUCSL and FEDFUNDS is not statistically significant (p > 0.15).

mechanism_narrative: The unemployment rate (UNRATE) at 11.1% is elevated, indicating labor market slack. When UNRATE rises by more than 0.3 percentage points in a single month, it signals accelerating job losses and weakening consumer confidence. This triggers negative S&P 500 returns over the next 5 trading days as investors immediately reprice recession risk and corporate earnings.
candidate_trigger: UNRATE increases by more than 0.3 percentage points month-over-month
regime_hint: labor market deterioration or recession onset
failure_condition: S&P 500 produces positive 5-day returns in more than 55% of months when UNRATE rises > 0.3pp, or correlation between UNRATE changes and 5-day returns is not significantly negative (p > 0.10).

mechanism_narrative: Real GDP growth (GDPC1) is the broadest measure of economic activity. When GDPC1 growth decelerates to below 2.0% annualized, it signals a weakening economic expansion. Combined with rising UNRATE, this interaction indicates a late-cycle slowdown that produces negative S&P 500 returns over 60 trading days as investors shift to defensive positioning.
candidate_trigger: GDPC1 annualized growth falls below 2.0% while UNRATE is above 5.0%
regime_hint: late-cycle slowdown with labor market weakness
failure_condition: S&P 500 produces positive 60-day returns in more than 55% of triggered instances, or the joint effect of GDPC1 growth and UNRATE on returns is not statistically significant (interaction p-value > 0.15).
